nativeRange.habitat: Reef caves, crevices, and ledges on coral reefs; generally found in shaded recesses and overhangs rather than open reef faces, often associated with cleaning stations.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves, ledges, and overhangs for daytime shelter
fine-grained substrate to protect long antennae

diet.notes: Generally considered a meaty-food carnivore and opportunistic scavenger. Has been documented eating frozen mysis and brine shrimp, finely chopped marine seafood, sinking pellets, and parasites or dead tissue removed from cooperative tankmates at cleaning stations. Iodine-rich foods and trace-element dosing tend to support successful moulting.

avoidWith: other Lysmata cleaner shrimp species (intra-genus aggression has been reported)

summary: Brilliant blood-red Indo-Pacific cleaner shrimp (4–5 cm) prized for its colour and parasite-picking behaviour. More reclusive and light-shy than the common Skunk Cleaner; functions as a hermaphrodite, so any two will pair.
careNotes: Generally considered a striking and useful reef-tank invertebrate, but noticeably more cryptic than the related Skunk Cleaner Shrimp (Lysmata amboinensis). Adults tend to stay tucked into rockwork caves and overhangs during the day, emerging more readily at dusk or under subdued lighting; aquarists who want to see their fire shrimp regularly are commonly advised to provide deep shaded recesses and avoid harsh overhead reef lighting directly above their hide. Cleaning behaviour is well documented — they will often set up cleaning stations and pick parasites, dead scales, and tissue from cooperative fish such as tangs and large angels. Like all marine shrimp, they are highly copper-sensitive and intolerant of copper-based medications; treat any diseased fish in a separate quarantine tank. Iodine and trace-element dosing within normal reef ranges is generally advised to support successful moults; shed exoskeletons can be left in the tank as a calcium source. Acclimation should be slow (drip method over 1–2 hours minimum) — they are sensitive to abrupt salinity and pH swings. Source ranges for temperature and salinity vary somewhat across hobby sites (Fish Laboratory and Aquarium Source: 72–78°F / SG 1.023–1.025; Aqua Imports: 74–78°F / 35 ppt ≈ SG 1.026); the values written here are an averaged middle. The supplied primary source (LiveAquaria category page) returned only navigation content with no species-specific care data, so Aqua Imports was promoted to primary-equivalent and cross-checked against Fish Laboratory and Aquarium Source to meet the two-source minimum.
breedingNotes: Has been bred occasionally in captivity but is generally considered difficult. All Lysmata are protandric simultaneous hermaphrodites — every individual matures first as a functional male and then develops functional female reproductive tissue while retaining male function, so any two adults can pair and produce viable eggs. Larvae are planktonic and pass through many zoeal stages over several weeks; survival to settlement requires a dedicated larval-rearing setup with continuous live food (rotifers, then enriched Artemia nauplii) and very stable parameters. Not practical in a standard display tank.

primary.name: Aqua Imports
primary.notes: Promoted to primary because the supplied LiveAquaria URL (a category page) contained no species-specific content.

additional.notes: Supplied primary source; URL resolved to a Diver's Den / category landing page with no Blood Red Fire Shrimp care data. Retained here for traceability only.

additional.name: Fish Laboratory
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~2 in / 5 cm), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025), 10-gal minimum tank, 2–5 year lifespan, hermaphroditism, copper sensitivity, and nocturnal/reclusive behaviour.

additional.name: Aquarium Source
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature 22–26°C, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.023–1.025, dKH 8–12, native depth 65–100 ft (~20–30 m), 2–3 year lifespan, and intra-genus aggression note (best kept singly or as a pair).

crustacean.moltingNotes: Healthy adults generally moult roughly every 4–6 weeks under stable conditions; juveniles moult more frequently. Shed exoskeletons can be left in the tank as a calcium source. Iodine-rich foods and normal reef trace-element dosing tend to support successful moults; sudden salinity, temperature, or alkalinity swings have been linked to failed moults.
crustacean.breedingNotes: All Lysmata are protandric simultaneous hermaphrodites — any two adults can pair and produce viable eggs. Larvae are planktonic and pass through many zoeal stages requiring continuous live food (rotifers, then enriched Artemia) and very stable water for several weeks; not practical in standard display tanks.
